title: TSMC 2026-Q2 results
gist: TSMC's strong Q2 2026 results led it to raise full-year 2026 revenue growth guidance from over 30% to slightly above 40% and lift 2026 capex to $60-64B (from ~$56B), driven by AI accelerator and agentic-AI CPU demand and the ramp of 2nm, even as mature-node (45/40nm, 28nm, 16nm) revenue declined sequentially.

themes.theme: Guidance and capex raise on AI demand

insights.claim: TSMC raised its full-year 2026 revenue growth outlook from more than 30% to slightly above 40%, citing stronger demand for AI accelerators and incremental CPU requirements tied to agentic AI workloads.
insights.speaker: user nl
insights.so_what: A ~10-point full-year revenue growth guidance raise mid-year is a strong bullish signal for AI-linked leading-edge foundry demand, directly relevant to TSMC valuation and the broader AI capex cycle.
insights.quote: The company raised its full-year 2026 revenue growth outlook from more than 30% to slightly above 40%, citing stronger demand for AI accelerators and incremental CPU requirements associated with agentic AI workloads.

insights.claim: TSMC increased its 2026 capital expenditure budget from approximately $56 billion to $60-64 billion, funding accelerated N3 capacity expansion including three new fabs in Taiwan, Arizona, and Japan, additional N5-to-N3 equipment conversion, and broader U.S. expansion; management attributed part of the increase to semiconductor equipment price inflation while saying there are no immediate tool-supply bottlenecks.
insights.speaker: user nl
insights.so_what: A $4-8B capex increase funding new fabs across three geographies is a concrete leading indicator of TSMC's confidence in sustained AI-driven demand and a direct order-flow signal for WFE suppliers (ASML, Applied Materials, etc.).
insights.quote: TSMC increased its 2026 capital expenditure budget from approximately $56 billion to $60–64 billion.

insights.claim: CFO Wendell Huang said Q3 2026 business will be supported by continued strong demand for leading-edge process technologies, including a steep ramp-up of TSMC's 2-nanometer technology.
insights.speaker: user nl
insights.so_what: Confirms 2nm is entering a steep production ramp in Q3 2026, a timing marker relevant to which chip customers (Apple, Nvidia, AMD, Qualcomm) will first benefit from 2nm capacity and yield improvement.
insights.quote: Moving into third quarter 2026, we expect our business to be supported by continued strong demand for our leading-edge process technologies, including the steep ramp-up of our 2-nanometer technology.

insights.claim: TSMC's gross margin remains resilient despite the early N2 ramp, with Q3 margin guided to 66% and high-60s percentage performance expected to continue, and pricing described as strategic rather than aggressively opportunistic.
insights.speaker: user nl
insights.so_what: A 66%+ gross margin sustained even during a new-node ramp (which typically pressures margins) signals unusually strong TSMC pricing power in the current AI-driven demand environment.
insights.quote: Gross margin remains resilient despite the early N2 ramp, with third-quarter margin guided to 66% and high-60% performance expected to continue.

insights.claim: Mature-node demand is TSMC's main area of weakness, with revenue declining sequentially across 45/40nm, 28nm, and 16nm nodes, with strength limited to power-management ICs and CMOS image sensors, potentially signaling pressure from elevated memory prices and broader semiconductor-cycle softness outside AI.
insights.speaker: user nl
insights.so_what: Sequential decline in legacy-node revenue is a signal that the current semiconductor upcycle is narrowly concentrated in AI/leading-edge demand, reinforcing the bifurcated 'ASP-led not volume-led' cycle thesis seen elsewhere in this dataset.
insights.quote: Revenue declined sequentially across 45/40nm, 28nm, and 16nm, with strength limited to power-management ICs and CMOS image sensors.
